class.name: Line3D
class.brief_description: A 3D line defined by a series of points.
class.description:
A 3D line defined by a series of points.
Line3D uses a LineMesh to render a line in 3D space. You can customize the appearance of the line using various properties, including width, color, gradient, and alignment.
To create a line, add points to the Line3D using the add_point() method or by setting the points property directly. You can also modify existing points using set_point_position().
The line can be set to be closed (i.e., the last point connects back to the first point) using the closed property. You can also enable or disable simplification of the line using the simplify property, which removes unnecessary points based on a specified tolerance.
The alignment property determines how the line is oriented in 3D space. It can be set to align to the view, face toward a specific position, or align to a specified normal vector.

method.name: add_point
method.description: Adds a point to the line at the specified position. If index is -1, the point is added at the end of the line.

method.name: clear_points
method.description: Removes all points from the line.

method.name: get_length
method.description: Returns the total length of the line. If properties affecting line length have been modified, the length is recalculated.

method.name: get_point_position
method.description: Returns the position of the point at the specified index.

method.name: remove_point
method.description: Removes the point at the specified index. If the index is out of range, an error is raised.

method.name: set_dirty
method.description: Marks the line as dirty, causing it to be redrawn on the next frame. This is useful if you have made multiple changes to the line and want to update its appearance. Note: that the line is automatically marked as dirty when you modify its properties or points, so you typically do not need to call this method directly.

method.name: set_point_position
method.description: Sets the position of the point at the specified index. If the index is out of range, an error is raised.

members.member: Determines how the line is oriented in 3D space.
How smooth the line caps are (higher values result in smoother caps).
Whether the line is closed (i.e., the last point connects back to the first point).
The base color of the line.
How smooth the line corners are (higher values result in smoother corners).
Whether to draw rounded caps at the ends of the line.
Whether to draw rounded corners at the points where the line changes direction.
A gradient to apply along the length of the line.
The normal vector used when alignment is set to ALIGN_TO_NORMAL.
The array of points defining the line.
Whether to simplify the line by removing unnecessary points. This can help improve performance for lines with many points.
The tolerance used when simplifying the line. Higher values result in more aggressive simplification. Only used if simplify is true.

Whether to use global space for the points instead of local space. When enabled, the points are interpreted in the global coordinate system, meaning they will not move with the Line3D node if it is transformed. This can be useful for creating lines that remain fixed in the world space regardless of the node's position or rotation.
The width of the line. This defines how thick the line appears in 3D space. You can also use the width_curve property to modulate the width along the length of the line.

A curve to modulate the width of the line along its length. The horizontal axis of the curve represents the normalized distance along the line (0.0 at the start, 1.0 at the end), while the vertical axis represents the width multiplier. For example, a value of 1.0 means the width is unchanged, while a value of 0.5 means the width is halved. This allows for creating lines that taper or vary in thickness along their length. If no curve is assigned, the line will have a constant width as defined by the width property.

constants.constant: Align the line segments to always face the camera.
Align the line segments to face toward a specific position in space.
Align the line segments to a specified normal vector.
